title: Quantum Orders, Rare Earth Retaliation, and the Anthropic Fallout
subhead: Trump signs two executive orders mandating post-quantum cryptography across federal systems by 2030-31, China strikes back at US rare earth companies, and the G7 scrambles for a framework on frontier AI access after the Anthropic export control debacle.

sections.heading: Trump Signs Quantum Orders: PQC Migration by 2031, Quantum Computer Push
sections.body:
President Trump signed two executive orders on June 22 that reshape the US government's posture toward quantum technology. The first, 'Securing the Nation Against Advanced Cryptographic Attacks', directs agencies to migrate high-value assets to post-quantum cryptography — key establishment by 2030 and digital signatures by 2031. The Office of Management and Budget and the National Cyber Director will lead the transition. A pilot PQC migration project must be completed by December 31, 2027. Contractors will be required to meet federal cybersecurity standards and vulnerability disclosure policies by the end of 2030.

The second order, 'Ushering In the Next Frontier of Quantum Innovation', establishes a national effort to build a cryptographically-relevant quantum computer at a Department of Energy facility. It expands the FBI's Quantum Information Science and Technology Counterintelligence Protection Team to study adversarial threats to the domestic quantum ecosystem. National Cyber Director Sean Cairncross said at the signing that 'innovation and security have to be balanced.' IBM CEO Arvind Krishna attended the ceremony.

Nextgov/FCW, which first reported the likely deadlines in May, notes that the orders come amid growing concern about 'harvest now, decrypt later' attacks — where adversaries collect encrypted data today with plans to break it once quantum computers arrive. The White House fact sheet references a June 2025 EO on cybersecurity and a March 2026 Cyber Strategy as prior building blocks.
sections.whyItMatters: The 2030/2031 deadlines are hard compliance dates for federal systems — the most aggressive PQC timeline any government has set. The counterintelligence expansion also signals that the US views quantum research as a domain of great-power competition, not just science policy.

sections.heading: China Retaliates on Rare Earths, Targeting US Supply Chain Builders
sections.body:
On June 22, China's Ministry of Commerce added 10 US entities to its export control list, including MP Materials and USA Rare Earth — the two companies at the center of Washington's effort to build a domestic rare earth supply chain. The restrictions bar Chinese exporters from shipping dual-use goods to these firms, and extend to third-party countries. Simultaneously, China's Finance Ministry announced government procurement bans on 46 US firms, including subsidiaries of Lockheed Martin, General Dynamics, and Raytheon — though analysts note these companies already cannot sell military hardware to Beijing under US law.

The Straits Times reports that analysts at Nanyang Technological University and the S. Rajaratnam School of International Studies characterize the moves as 'calibrated' — noting that Nvidia and other major chipmakers were spared, and that the targeted defense firms have limited reliance on China. But the inclusion of MP Materials and USA Rare Earth is deliberate: the Pentagon became MP Materials' largest shareholder in July 2025 with a roughly 15 percent stake, and acquired 10 percent of USA Rare Earth in January as part of a $1.6 billion investment.

The timing is pointed. Just four days earlier, the Pentagon's Office of Strategic Capital announced a $725 million conditional loan to Energy Fuels to build domestic rare earth metallization capacity — the step that converts oxides into the metals and alloys used in F-35 actuators, Virginia-class submarine motors, and Tomahawk guidance packages. A separate $500 million commitment went to Phoenix Tailings for a 'Freedom Facility' using molten-salt electrochemistry. Together, $1.2 billion in one week. Tech Times reports that under federal procurement law (10 U.S.C. §4872, implemented via DFARS 252.225-7052), defense contractors must certify that no stage of rare earth magnet production occurred in China, Russia, Iran, or North Korea starting January 1, 2027 — six months away.
sections.whyItMatters: China controls over 70 percent of rare earth production and nearly 90 percent of refining capacity. By targeting the specific companies the US is subsidizing to break that dependency, Beijing is signalling it knows exactly where America's emerging alternative supply chain is most vulnerable — even if the immediate economic impact is limited.

sections.heading: The Anthropic Fallout: Export Controls, G7 Access Talks, and the Securitisation Debate
sections.body:
The reverberations from the US Commerce Department's June 12 export control directive on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models continue to spread across three separate but interlocking tracks.

The trigger. According to reporting by The Economist, cited by TechSpot, Senator Mark Warner disclosed that General Joshua Rudd — who leads both the NSA and US Cyber Command — told him Anthropic's Mythos model 'broke into almost all of our classified systems, not in weeks, but in hours' during an authorized red-team exercise. That Senate briefing came one day before Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick's letter to Anthropic suspending all foreign-national access. The government cited a jailbreak of Fable 5's safety classifiers, reported to the Commerce Department by Amazon — both a major Anthropic investor and an AI competitor. Trump later told Axios he no longer views Anthropic as a national security threat, but the export directive has not been rescinded.

The G7 angle. Reuters reports that at the G7 summit in France, leaders discussed granting 'trusted partners' access to cutting-edge US AI models. French President Emmanuel Macron said he expects progress in coming weeks on broadening access to Anthropic's Mythos specifically. The RUSI analysis, titled 'Gatekeeping the Frontier: When AI Access Becomes a National Security Concern,' argues that the sequence — from the June 2 White House EO mandating a voluntary US-government exclusive preview of 'covered models,' to the export directive on Anthropic — represents 'the direct expression of the grip the US can exert on any AI lab that gets too deep into opening up capabilities to general public.' RUSI also notes that the UK rebranded its AI Safety Institute to an AI Security Institute, and the US reconstituted its Center for AI Standards and Innovation around national security.

The broader pattern. Forbes contributor Nili Gilbert writes that the US blocking foreign access to Anthropic's models has governments worldwide racing to build sovereign AI. Anthropic itself argued that similar jailbreak vulnerabilities exist in other publicly available models, including OpenAI's GPT-5.5, which are not subject to comparable controls. Former Facebook CSO Alex Stamos said the findings were valid but did not justify the severity of the response.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first time export controls have been applied to an AI model, and the mechanisms are still being improvised. The RUSI piece makes the structural point clearly: access to frontier AI is being reclassified from a commercial question to a national security question, narrowing the circle of decision-makers and reducing the scope for public debate.

sections.heading: US Holds Off Blacklisting DeepSeek, Even as Over 100 Firms Flagged
sections.body:
Reuters reports that the Trump administration has held off adding China's AI startup DeepSeek and more than 100 other companies that have been flagged as national security risks to the Commerce Department's Entity List. The decision reflects an effort to avoid escalating tensions with Beijing following the Trump-Xi summit in May, where the two leaders agreed on a framework for a 'constructive and strategically stable' relationship over the next three years.

Memory chipmaker CXMT is among the firms that would have been listed. The hold mirrors a broader pattern: the administration is selectively applying trade restrictions — moving aggressively on Anthropic and rare earths supply chains while exercising restraint on additional Chinese entity listings — suggesting that the current approach prioritizes diplomatic channels over broad-spectrum economic decoupling.
sections.whyItMatters: The gap between firms flagged and firms actually listed gives Washington ongoing leverage — but it also means that the most consequential Chinese AI companies remain free to operate in global markets, even as the US tightens controls on model access from the American side.

sections.heading: SK Hynix Overtakes Samsung as Korea's Most Valuable Company
sections.body:
SK Hynix surpassed Samsung Electronics in market capitalization on Monday for the first time since 2000, reaching roughly $1.35 trillion — narrowly edging Samsung's approximately $1.349 trillion. Reuters reports that SK Hynix shares have risen more than 340 percent in 2026, compared with roughly 200 percent for Samsung.

The milestone reflects the structural shift driven by AI memory demand. SK Hynix has positioned itself as the dominant supplier of high-bandwidth memory (HBM) chips paired with Nvidia's AI accelerators — a segment where Samsung has lagged. Firstpost notes that SK Hynix reported a record operating profit of 23.5 trillion won in 2024, a dramatic reversal from the 7.73 trillion won operating loss in 2023.

The crossover also makes SK Hynix the world's most valuable memory-chip maker, surpassing US rival Micron Technology. It is a striking turnaround for a company that traded at 135 won per share in 2003 and nearly sold itself to Micron before the deal collapsed.
sections.whyItMatters: The memory chip market is no longer cyclical-commodity — it is strategic infrastructure. SK Hynix's ascent shows that AI has elevated memory manufacturers into power brokers, and the company's dominance in HBM gives both it and South Korea significant leverage in the AI supply chain.

sections.heading: Rocket Lab Shatters Responsive Space Record in VICTUS HAZE Mission
sections.body:
Rocket Lab launched the US Space Force's VICTUS HAZE mission on June 19 from Mahia, New Zealand, going from formal notice-to-launch to liftoff in 16 hours and 42 minutes — beating the mission's 24-hour requirement. SpaceNews reports that the company's guidance, navigation, and control team calculated a final trajectory to a previously undisclosed orbit, updated flight software, and coordinated ground stations in roughly four hours.

Once in orbit, Rocket Lab completed spacecraft commissioning in 37 hours and 36 minutes, more than 34 hours ahead of the 72-hour operational deadline. The spacecraft will now conduct rendezvous and proximity operations with True Anomaly's Jackal-004, already in orbit since May 3, to demonstrate space domain awareness and characterization capabilities.

VICTUS HAZE is the fourth demonstration under the Space Force's Tactically Responsive Space program. The 2023 predecessor, Victus Nox, proved rapid launch in 27 hours. This mission extends the concept to on-orbit maneuvering and inspection — capabilities relevant to countering adversarial satellites and replacing damaged assets on timelines measured in days rather than months. Col. Bryon McClain, the Space Force's acting portfolio acquisition executive for Space Combat Power, said the exercise demonstrates the ability 'to respond to irresponsible behavior on orbit under operationally realistic conditions.'
sections.whyItMatters: Sub-24-hour launch and sub-48-hour on-orbit commissioning compresses the timeline for military space operations significantly. If responsive launch becomes operational rather than experimental, it changes the calculus for satellite warfare — rapid replacement and rapid inspection become feasible.

bottomLine: Three tracks converged this week: the US set hard deadlines for quantum-resistant cryptography and pushed domestic rare earth metallization with $1.2 billion in Pentagon loans, China targeted those same rare earth companies with calibrated export controls, and the first-ever export controls on an AI model continued to ripple through allied capitals at the G7. Separately, SK Hynix's market-value overtaking of Samsung confirmed that AI memory has become strategic infrastructure, and Rocket Lab's 16-hour responsive launch demonstrated that military space operations can now happen on timelines previously reserved for crisis response. The common thread: governments are moving from studying technology-power dynamics to setting hard deadlines and spending real money.
sources: Reuters, RUSI, Straits Times, TechSpot, White House Fact Sheet, Nextgov/FCW, SpaceNews, Firstpost, Forbes, AP News, Seeking Alpha, Tech Times, Gizmodo, The Economist
